title: Client fetch lifecycle

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Client fetch lifecycle; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Client fetch lifecycle
sources.claim: React data fetching needs ownership, cache and freshness policy, cancellation, race handling, and explicit UI states.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React data fetching needs ownership, cache and freshness policy, cancellation, race handling, and explicit UI states.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Changing learnerId aborts or ignores the prior request before its stale response can update current state. Your goal is to implement a fetch-backed component with cancellation and retry behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Cleanup and race prevention; javascript network fetch; Loading, empty, success and failure states. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React data fetching needs ownership, cache and freshness policy, cancellation, race handling, and explicit UI states. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Effects can duplicate requests, frameworks may fetch elsewhere, and later responses can overwrite newer intent. This draft therefore rejects: A fetch effect needs no cleanup because React ignores every response after props change.

includes: Trigger bounded requests, validate responses and prevent obsolete results from winning.
Implement a fetch-backed component with cancellation and retry behavior.
excludes: Effects can duplicate requests, frameworks may fetch elsewhere, and later responses can overwrite newer intent.

outcomes: Implement a fetch-backed component with cancellation and retry behavior.

examples.title: Client fetch lifecycle: concrete case
examples.situation: Changing learnerId aborts or ignores the prior request before its stale response can update current state.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Changing learnerId aborts or ignores the prior request before its stale response can update current state.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Cancel or ignore obsolete requests and prevent stale responses from updating state for newer inputs.
examples.result: The result follows because Cancel or ignore obsolete requests and prevent stale responses from updating state for newer inputs.
